core_intuitions: Gradient signal per prompt scales as p(1-p): it peaks at pass rate 0.5 and vanishes at both ends, so an all-correct or all-wrong group is dead weight that produced zero advantage despite full rollout cost.
Pass rate is non-stationary — a property of the prompt AND the current policy together — so difficulty is a moving target that must be tracked online (EMA / decayed Beta), never labelled once.

Difficulty-targeted selection (before generation) and dynamic sampling (after generation) attack zero-variance groups from opposite ends of the loop: selection raises the survival fraction rho so you oversample less; dynamic sampling drops the dead groups and refills, guaranteeing every gradient step trains on signal.
Curriculum is emergent, not authored: keep selecting for the p≈0.5 band and the band's contents drift from easy to hard automatically as the policy masters material.

Replay is three different objects with three different correctness constraints: an on-policy prompt buffer (free, the curriculum), a shallow off-policy staleness buffer (importance-corrected, half-life of a few steps because IS weights blow up and clipping zeroes stale gradients), and a success/hard-case buffer (anti-forgetting).

existing.name: rldata-signal-parabola
existing.issue: The p(1-p) signal parabola with p*=0.5 peak (Var=0.25), the p=0.9 point (Var=0.09) and the near-zero p=0.99 'dead' point, plus the 2.8x-signal annotation and the all-wrong/all-correct A_i=0 endpoints. Correct, single-peaked, and both themes render cleanly with legible labels and good contrast.

existing.name: rldata-curriculum-band-drift
existing.issue: Two label problems hurt legibility, especially in dark theme: 'band target (p*)' overlaps the green (easy) curve near its 0.5 crossing, and 'band rides where tiers cross p*' overlaps the blue (medium) curve. Conceptually the fixed vertical shaded band sits on the far left while the three circular markers at the p*=0.5 crossings are what actually embody 'the band' — the relationship between the static shaded strip and the three sliding crossing points is not visually spelled out, so a reader can misread the strip as the whole story.
existing.improvement: Move both text labels into clear whitespace (e.g. drop 'band target (p*)' onto the dashed p*=0.5 line in the lower-left gutter, and place 'band rides where tiers cross p*' below the curves) so no label crosses a curve in either theme. Tie the three crossing markers together explicitly — e.g. a faint shaded band or connective guide that visibly slides left-to-right through the easy, then medium, then hard crossing of p*=0.5 — so it is unmistakable that the training band is the region riding the diagonal where successive tiers cross the target, not the one fixed strip on the left.

existing.name: rldata-sample-efficiency-pipeline
existing.issue: The six-stage vertical pipeline (raw pool -> verifiable corpus -> usable corpus w/ priors -> selected batch -> raw groups -> informative training batch -> prioritized prompt buffer) with the expensive generation step highlighted, the GRPO-update branch, and the feedback loop 'feeds back into (3) = emergent curriculum'. Reading order is clear, the numbered annotations map to chapter stages, and both themes render legibly.

new_figures.name: rldata-dynamic-sampling-sieve
new_figures.concept: Selection-before + zero-variance-filter-after: how the two mechanisms jointly keep every trained group informative, and what the oversampling tax / survival fraction rho actually is.
new_figures.rationale: This is the operational heart of the chapter and the one core mechanism no existing figure shows. The parabola gives the WHY (dead groups), the pipeline lists it as one box, but neither shows the actual loop: pick in-band prompts, generate G samples, watch some groups come back all-same (dead), sieve them out, and oversample to refill the batch. The entire worked example (the ~2x -> ~1.37x oversampling-tax saving) is pure arithmetic in the text; a picture of groups-as-columns-of-dots surviving or dying makes rho and the select/filter division of labor click instantly.
new_figures.placement: Dynamic sampling: never train on a zero-variance batch
new_figures.brief: Show the two-ends-of-the-loop mechanism as a horizontal flow with three columns. LEFT column 'select (before)': a large prompt pool drawn as a scatter of small tokens spread across a difficulty axis (label the axis low-p ... p*=0.5 ... high-p); the difficulty-targeted selector picks a subset clustered near the p*=0.5 band (highlight the band). MIDDLE column 'generate G per prompt': render each selected prompt as a short vertical column of G=8 small circles (use a neutral illustrative G like 8), each circle filled to mean 'pass' vs hollow/outline to mean 'fail'. Show three visually distinct group types: an all-filled column (all-correct), an all-hollow column (all-wrong), and mixed columns (some filled some hollow). RIGHT column 'filter (after) = dynamic sampling': a sieve/gate that lets MIXED groups through to an 'informative training batch' and drops the two zero-variance types (all-filled and all-wrong) into a discard tray labelled 'zero-variance: dropped'. From the discard, draw a curved 'oversample to refill' arrow back to the generate step, with a small counter reading 'survival fraction rho = kept / generated'. Include a compact caption strip: 'selection raises rho (fewer dead groups made); dynamic sampling guarantees rho<1 batches are still fully informative.' Do NOT hardcode benchmark rho values — use a small illustrative fraction like 'kept 6 / generated 9' drawn from the dots actually shown, so the picture is self-consistent. Animation (enhancement only; the static first frame must already read): groups stream through generation, the all-same ones peel off into the discard tray, the refill arrow fires and a fresh wave generates until the kept batch fills to target; a rho meter ticks up as informative groups accumulate. Theme-safe: pass/fail encoded by fill vs outline plus shape, not by red/green alone; no hardcoded primary colors; everything inside the viewBox with no overlapping labels.

new_figures.name: rldata-three-buffers
new_figures.concept: The three replay buffers are distinct objects with distinct correctness constraints: on-policy prompt buffer (free), shallow off-policy staleness buffer (importance-corrected, short half-life), success/hard-case buffer (retention).
new_figures.rationale: 'Three buffers, three jobs' is stated as a practitioner tip AND a Key Takeaway, and the whole replay section hinges on NOT conflating them — yet it is entirely text. Practitioners routinely collapse these into one mental 'replay buffer' and get the correctness wrong (replaying stale completions without importance weighting, or expecting a DQN-style deep reservoir). A side-by-side comparison card that contrasts WHAT each stores, its on/off-policy status, its correctness requirement, and its purpose is exactly the kind of taxonomy a static infographic nails.
new_figures.placement: Replay buffers: reusing rollouts you already paid for
new_figures.brief: A three-panel comparison card, one column per buffer, with a shared row structure so the columns are directly comparable. Column 1 'Prompt buffer (which tasks to attempt)': stores TASK ids / prompts only (never completions); on-policy (completions always freshly generated under current policy); correctness = trivially unbiased, FREE; purpose = throughput + signal, this IS the curriculum; depth = the whole live pool. Column 2 'Staleness buffer (recent completions)': stores actual completions plus their stored old log-probs pi_old; off-policy (rollouts are 1-4 policy-versions stale); correctness = MUST importance-correct with the PPO ratio and clip; depth = SHALLOW, a half-life of a few steps — evict beyond staleness bound tau_max, monitor clipped-token fraction; purpose = async latency overlap. Column 3 'Success / hard-case buffer (banked winners)': stores successful trajectories on rarely-solved tasks; used as on-policy-ish positive / SFT-style anchors; correctness = expert-iteration style, retention not gradient-unbiasedness; purpose = anti-forgetting on sparse agentic tasks. Draw a small icon per column that captures the essence: a stack of prompt cards; a short conveyor with a decaying/fading tail (the half-life); a vault/lockbox of banked trophies. Add one crisp warning banner under column 2 only: 'stored trajectories have a half-life — stale = clipped = zero gradient'. Use a consistent labelled row down the left edge ('stores', 'policy status', 'correctness', 'depth', 'job') so the reader scans across. Theme-safe, no invented numbers beyond the illustrative tau_max ~ 2–4 already in the chapter; everything legible inside the viewBox, no overlapping text.

new_figures.name: rldata-replay-halflife
new_figures.concept: Why a stored trajectory decays: as the policy drifts, the importance ratio moves away from 1, the clip window closes on it, and the trajectory's gradient contribution falls to zero — hence a shallow buffer and a staleness eviction bound.
new_figures.rationale: The phrase 'staleness is the half-life of a stored trajectory' is one of the chapter's sharpest intuitions but is delivered only in prose plus a clip formula. A picture of the clip window closing as staleness grows makes concrete WHY trajectory replay must be shallow (a few steps) rather than a deep DQN reservoir — the single most common misconception when porting replay from value-based RL to on-policy LLM RL. Priority 3 because it refines a point the three-buffers card already introduces; include if the chapter can support a fourth figure.
new_figures.brief: A left-to-right decay diagram along a 'staleness (policy versions since generation)' axis from tau=0 to tau=tau_max and beyond. At each staleness step show a small distribution/dot for the importance ratio rho_t = pi_theta / pi_old: at tau=0 it sits right at 1.0 (inside the clip band); as tau grows the ratio drifts away from 1 and its spread widens. Overlay the fixed PPO clip band [1-eps, 1+eps] as a horizontal shaded corridor centred on 1.0. Show that early (fresh) trajectories fall inside the corridor and carry gradient, while stale trajectories drift outside it where clip flattens the objective and the gradient contribution -> 0 (draw a small 'gradient contribution' bar under each staleness step, tall near tau=0 and shrinking to nothing by tau_max). Mark the eviction bound tau_max with a gate labelled 'evict beyond tau_max' and a trash icon for anything past it. Add a secondary readout: 'health metric = fraction of tokens clipped' rising as staleness grows. Small caption: 'shallow buffer, not a million-transition reservoir — staleness is the trajectory's half-life.' Use only the illustrative tau_max ~ 2–4 and eps already implied by the chapter; no benchmark numbers. Theme-safe, all elements inside the viewBox, no overlapping labels, understandable as a single static frame.

summary: The chapter is already served by three solid figures — the p(1-p) signal parabola (keep), the emergent-curriculum band-drift plot (refine: fix dark-theme label/curve overlaps and make the sliding band visually tie to the three p*-crossings), and the six-stage sample-efficiency pipeline (keep). The biggest un-illustrated core intuition is the dynamic-sampling loop itself — select-in-band-before / drop-zero-variance-after / oversample-to-refill, with survival fraction rho — which is currently only arithmetic in the worked example; that is the priority-1 new figure (animated sieve). The 'three buffers, three jobs' taxonomy is stated as both a tip and a Key Takeaway yet is text-only and easily conflated, warranting a priority-2 static comparison card. A priority-3 static figure on trajectory-replay half-life (clip window closing as staleness grows) would sharpen the 'why shallow, not a deep reservoir' point if a fourth figure is warranted.
